coredata.dc:title: The significant role of Z-scheme band alignment at the heterojunction for the enhanced photocatalytic H2 production in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite

coredata.dc:description: An efficient ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was prepared by a simple wet impregnation process. PXRD confirmed the tetragonal and orthorhombic crystalline natures of titanium dioxide (TiO2) and bismuth niobate (BiNbO4) respectively. The loading of BiNbO4 and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) shifted the light absorption of TiO2 to a longer wavelength from 400 to 430 nm. The suitable conduction band position of BiNbO4 facilitated a favourable band alignment to suppress the electron/hole (e−/h+) recombination in TiO2 via the Z scheme mechanism at the heterojunction. The excited electrons at the CB of TiO2 were easily transported via the fast electron accepting and conducting rGO matrix for the surface redox reactions with the improved charge transfer efficiency, which was confirmed by EIS and PL studies respectively. BiNbO4 and rGO synergistically improved the specific surface area and solar light absorption. EPR analysis confirmed the presence of increased oxygen vacancies at the heterojunction. Hence, the ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite demonstrated an enhanced hydrogen evolution (8910 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) than bare TiO2 (4820 μ mol h−1 gcat −1), bare BiNbO4 (950 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) and TiO2/BiNbO4 (6730 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) under direct solar light. The optimum of 1 wt % BiNbO4 and rGO loaded TiO2 nanocomposite produced twice the H2 production than the bare TiO2. This also further confirms that rGO played a major role during the photocatalytic process by highlighting the potential of metal oxides combined with carbon material as an efficient photocatalyst which prominently enhances the H2 evolution.

head.ce:title: The significant role of Z-scheme band alignment at the heterojunction for the enhanced photocatalytic H2 production in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was prepared by a simple wet impregnation process. PXRD confirmed the tetragonal and orthorhombic crystalline natures of titanium dioxide (TiO2) and bismuth niobate (BiNbO4) respectively. The loading of BiNbO4 and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) shifted the light absorption of TiO2 to a longer wavelength from 400 to 430 nm. The suitable conduction band position of BiNbO4 facilitated a favourable band alignment to suppress the electron/hole (e−/h+) recombination in TiO2 via the Z scheme mechanism at the heterojunction. The excited electrons at the CB of TiO2 were easily transported via the fast electron accepting and conducting rGO matrix for the surface redox reactions with the improved charge transfer efficiency, which was confirmed by EIS and PL studies respectively. BiNbO4 and rGO synergistically improved the specific surface area and solar light absorption. EPR analysis confirmed the presence of increased oxygen vacancies at the heterojunction. Hence, the ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite demonstrated an enhanced hydrogen evolution (8910 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) than bare TiO2 (4820 μ mol h−1 gcat −1), bare BiNbO4 (950 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) and TiO2/BiNbO4 (6730 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) under direct solar light. The optimum of 1 wt % BiNbO4 and rGO loaded TiO2 nanocomposite produced twice the H2 production than the bare TiO2. This also further confirms that rGO played a major role during the photocatalytic process by highlighting the potential of metal oxides combined with carbon material as an efficient photocatalyst which prominently enhances the H2 evolution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the growing energy demand and depletion of natural resources have increased the need for alternate energy resources [1]. It is mainly due to the ignition of natural fuels such as coal and petroleum as essential means to meet the energy needs for survival. In consideration of the ever-growing requirement for clean energy, significant efforts are being directed toward developments in the domain of energy research [2,3]. With a focus on addressing the issue faced by the environment and in the utilization of energy, there is an urge to find other possible ways to explore eco-friendly renewable resources. Hydrogen (H2) is a highly promising and environmentally friendly energy carrier, releasing only water as a by-product without any carbon emissions. It has numerous appealing attributes as an energy carrier, including a significantly high energy density of 140 MJ/kg, exceeding typical solid fuels (50 MJ/kg) by over two-fold. The global H2 production amounts to approximately 500 billion cubic meters (b m3) annually [4]. About, 96 % of the world's H2 production stems from non-renewable fossil fuels, primarily through the process of steam reforming methane. Nonetheless, this reliance on fossil fuels yields H2 of lower purity and a substantial release of detrimental greenhouse gases. Hence, there is a growing urgency to develop sustainable energy solutions that minimize the environmental impact. These solutions should aim to reduce carbon emissions and be incorporated with novel strategies for clean energy production [4,5]. The optimal approach to sustainable energy resources involves H2 production through water splitting via the photocatalytic method. This technique is not only environmentally friendly but also proves to be a cost-effective solution [6].

TiO2 has been employed as the most traditional photocatalyst for several years due to its outstanding stability, non-toxic nature and cost-effectiveness. However, the electron/hole (e−/h+) recombination rate and its wide band gap (3.2 eV) highly suppress the photocatalytic activity in the visible region during the water-splitting process [7,8]. Various attempts have been made to solve the issues of TiO2 by doping metals and non-metals, loading metal oxides and forming heterojunctions with co-catalysts [9–11]. Over the aforementioned techniques, the formation of heterojunctions with two or three semiconductors has emerged as a promising approach, which achieves maximal efficiency by suppressing the electron/hole (e−/h+) recombination and by tuning the band gap energies to the visible region [12–14]. Among them, developing the photocatalyst with staggered heterojunction has been demonstrated as the most efficient strategy attributed to its outstanding facilitation in the separation of charge carriers [15,16]. This type of heterojunction also tunes the band gap to use maximal sunlight in the visible region [17].

In recent times, bismuth-based photocatalysts have drawn attention due to their extraordinary properties such as being highly stable, low toxic and exhibiting high photocatalytic response [18]. Among various types of bismuth-based semiconductors, niobate shows high efficiency in different applications such as ferroelectrics [19], photocatalysis [20,21] and luminescence [22] owing to its distinct properties. BiNbO4 exists in different crystalline structures at two different temperatures. Orthorhombic and triclinic polymorph synthesized at respective low and high temperatures exist with contrasting properties [23]. The previously conducted study reported that the photocatalytic response of orthorhombic was higher than the triclinic structure among these two polymorphs [24]. It is important to develop visible light-responsive photocatalysts to attain the maximum utilization of the solar spectrum. BiNbO4 has a band gap of 2.9 eV, which makes it a visible responsive photocatalytic material. The CB position of BiNbO4 is not suitable for efficient H2 production, whereas its CB position is more suitable for suppressing the e−/h+ recombination in TiO2. Hence it can be used as a co-catalyst to improve the efficiency of TiO2 [25,26]. Constructing the heterojunction between semiconductors can be beneficial to prevent exciton recombination, which increases the photocatalytic activity [27].

The usage of carbon materials improves the charge mobility, surface area and adsorption sites for H2 production. In addition, it can tune the band gap and can potentially receive the electrons, which further facilitates the charge carrier separation. Some of the carbon materials such as graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes and their derivatives, etc., were used as support materials [28]. The honeycomb structure of graphene with a two-dimensional structure, sp2 hybridization exists with exceptional physicochemical properties such as flexibility, elasticity, high resistance, transparency and non-toxic in nature [29,30]. In addition to this, it is highly stable and cost-effective. Even though with high stability, graphene cannot be used directly because of its zero-band gap. It could be synthesized by various methods with a tuned band gap by the adulteration of heteroatoms on the surface to enhance the catalytic properties of graphene. The electron density of graphene changes when it is doped with heteroatoms and on hydrogenation, the hybridization changes to sp3 and it increases the band gap. Owing to its electron-accepting properties, it can act as a cocatalyst and can improve photocatalytic activity [31]. Graphene oxide (GO) can be synthesized by enormous methods by exfoliating or by chemical oxidation. Based on pioneering work, modified Hummer's methods were mostly employed for the synthesis of GO, as reported earlier [32,33]. GO with excellent optical, chemical and electrical properties could be employed as a basic material for producing reduced graphene oxide [34]. The rGO and its electron-conducting nature facilitate the excitons generation and increase the charge mobility [35,36]. The rGO acts as a support matrix between two semiconductors and enhances the reactions on the surface owing to its unique properties.

In the current research work, the ternary nanocomposite TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was developed by a wet impregnation technique and its photocatalyst efficacy was evaluated for H2 production. The loading concentration of BiNbO4 and rGO on TiO2 was optimized to improve the photocatalytic performance under solar light. The performance and efficacy of the system were considerably improved by the synergistic contribution of rGO and BiNbO4 with enhanced charge transfer efficiency and exciton life. Based on the literature survey, there is no report on TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite photocatalyst for H2 production via the water-splitting process.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide, bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate, niobium chloride, triblock copolymer [poly (ethylene glycol)-block-poly (propylene glycol)-block poly (ethylene glycol)] (Pluronic P-123), nitric acid, graphite powder, ethanol and distilled water. All the above chemicals were purchased under analytical grade and used without further purification.

TiO2 was synthesized by employing a hydrothermal reaction. The stoichiometric amount of titanium (IV) isopropoxide and a mesoporous structure-directing template polymer P-123 were dissolved in ethanol separately and stirred for 3 h. After 3 h, titanium (IV) isopropoxide solution was transferred into P-123 solution with continuous stirring of 30 min. Further, 60 ml of distilled water was added to the above solution and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. The above suspension was introduced for ultrasonication and transferred into a teflon-sealed autoclave. The autoclave was kept in a hot air oven for 5 h at 120 °C. After the hydrothermal process, the obtained products were washed with distilled water and ethanol several times and kept for drying at 100 °C in a hot air oven until it dried. The dried sample was ground using mortar and pestle and calcined for 5 h at 400 °C. The schematic representation for the synthesis of mesoporous TiO2 is shown in Fig. 1 a.

BiNbO4 nanoparticle was synthesized by adapting the sol-gel technique. 0.64 g of niobium chloride and 1.14 g of bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate were dissolved in ethanol separately with constant stirring for 30 min at 100 °C. The above two solutions were mixed and 2 ml of nitric acid was added under constant stirring for 30 min. Finally, the above suspensions were kept for drying in a hot air oven for 24 h at 90 °C and calcined at 700 °C for 10 h at a rate of 2 °C/min. The diagrammatic representation for the fabrication of BiNbO4 is shown in Fig. 1b.

The TiO2/BiNbO4 nanocomposites were prepared by employing a simple wet impregnation method. The stoichiometrically calculated amount of BiNbO4 was added separately into the beakers containing the required amount of TiO2 in ethanol for the different weight percentages (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 wt %) of BiNbO4-loaded TiO2 (TiO2/BiNbO4). To achieve thorough dispersion, the above mixtures were sonicated for 30 min and kept for 2 h stirring. Subsequently, the homogenous mixed samples were obtained and dried completely at 100 °C in a hot air oven. The dried TiO2/BiNbO4 samples were finely ground and stored. The schematic approach for the preparation process of TiO2/BiNbO4 is shown in Fig. 1c.
Graphene oxide was prepared by the modified hummers method followed by thermal reduction at 400 °C for 2 h of GO to obtain rGO [33].

The TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposites were developed using a wet impregnation method. The stoichiometrically required amount of rGO for the preparation of various weight percentages of rGO (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 wt %) loaded TiO2/BiNbO4 (TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO) was added individually into the beakers containing the calculated amount of TiO2/BiNbO4 in ethanol. To ensure even distribution, the mixtures were sonicated for 30 min and stirred for 2 h. The homogenous mixtures were dried completely at 100 °C in a hot air oven. The preparation process of ternary nanocomposite TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO is illustrated in Fig. 1c.

The X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D2 Phaser desktop, Germany) was utilized for the phase characterization of synthesized photocatalyst using CuKα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm) as the X-ray source. The catalyst functional groups were examined in a Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectrometer using the KBr pellet technique (Perkins Elmer). Raman spectra were recorded in the range of 50–4000 cm−1 in BRUKER RFS 27 MultiRAM FT Raman Spectrometer using LN2 cooled ultra-high sensitivity Ge detector with Nd-YAG laser frequency of 1064 nm. The morphological nature, particle size and the elements present in the photocatalyst were examined by High-Resolution Scanning Electron Microscopy (HR-SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) (FEI QUANTA 200F HESEM) with ETD and LFD detectors at a high voltage of 30 KV respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were captured (JEOL Japan JEM-2100 Plus) at 200 KV equipped with a Gatan digital camera. For Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy, the synthesized materials were subjected to scanning within the 200–800 nm range using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu model 2450, Japan) equipped with an integrating sphere, while barium sulfate (BaSO4) was employed as the reference substance. The specific surface area was determined through the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method and the pore size distribution was computed using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The chemical states of the elements present in the nanocomposite were analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS- PHI- VERSAPROBE III) using a monochromatic X-ray beam source of 15 μm. The Photoluminescence spectra were recorded at the Varian Cary Eclipse Photo Luminescence Spectrophotometer. The electrochemical impedance studies were carried out under a three-electrode assembly setup using 0.1 M sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) electrolyte solution with platinum wire and Ag/AgCl as counter and reference electrodes. The electron spin resonance spectra were recorded at the JEOL – JES X320 spectrometer.

The efficacy of the developed photocatalysts was evaluated for H2 evolution via the water-splitting technique under direct solar light. The solar intensity measured during the reaction was around 90,000 Lux (±5 %) using Digital Luxmeter (Lutron, LX-1102). The photocatalytic experiment was carried out in a quartz reactor containing 47.5 ml of aqueous medium with 5 mg of photocatalyst and 2.5 ml of glycerol as a hole scavenger agent. Before proceeding, the reaction mixture was purged with nitrogen gas under dark conditions to remove the dissolved oxygen and air. To achieve the uniform dispersion of photocatalyst the reaction was carried out under constant stirring, after 4 h of reaction the produced H2 was analyzed with gas chromatography (Shimadzu GC-2014 with Molecular Sieve/5 Å) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector and nitrogen as carrier gas. The stability of the photocatalyst was evaluated under the aforementioned experimental conditions by reusing the same photocatalyst in four cycles [37].

In Fig. 2 , Powder X-ray diffraction confirmed the crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized photocatalysts. The different diffraction peaks in Fig. 2a with 2θ values of 25.35°, 37.75°, 48.04°, 53.98°, 55.03°, 62.57°, 68.74°, 70.08°, 75.04° and 82.52° were highly indexed to the crystal planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), (215) and (224) of anatase TiO2 respectively, which was confirmed with JCPDS card no: 21–1272. This shows the presence of pure anatase TiO2 phase, not other rutile and brookite phases. In Fig. 2d, the PXRD pattern of orthorhombic BiNbO4 demonstrated that major diffraction peaks at 15.17°, 19.38°, 23.79°, 25.01°, 28.98°, 30.24°, 31.21°, 33.00°, 37.67°, 49.59°, and 51.72° corresponding to the crystal planes (020), (110), (101), (111), (121), (040), (002), (131), (112), (212) and (103), which matches with JCPDS card no. 16–0295. The PXRD pattern in Fig. 2e showed the diffraction peaks at 25.83° and 43.79° indexed with (002) and (111) hkl planes of rGO [38]. There was no evident change and shift in the PXRD patterns of TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO in Fig. 2b and c, due to the low concentration of BiNbO4 and rGO. The Debye Scherrer formula, D = Kλ/β cos θ was used to determine the average crystallite size of the synthesized photocatalyst, where K is the Scherrer constant (0.89), D is the average particle size (in nm), θ is the Bragg's angle, λ is the wavelength of the X-ray source (0.1542 nm) and β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM). The crystallite size of anatase TiO2 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was calculated from the high-intensity diffraction peak as 10.30 and 10.05 nm respectively, and a slight decrease in the crystallite size of TiO2 was noted. The presence of BiNbO4 in TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was confirmed by the low intense peaks observed at 2θ of 28.98° shown in magnified Fig. 2f. It is expected that the predominant peak of rGO at 25.83° must have been merged with the primary peak of anatase TiO2 at 25.35°, hence the PXRD pattern of rGO was not observed distinctly.

The FT-IR spectra of TiO2, BiNbO4, rGO, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO are shown in Fig. 3 . In the spectra, a, b and c, the band region around 1600 cm−1 denoted the bending vibration of hydroxyl groups and the band around 500-750 cm−1 was obtained for the bending mode (O–Ti–O) of anatase TiO2 [39]. The peak occurred near 852 and 607 cm−1 for the BiNbO4 spectrum in Fig. 3d, confirming the stretching vibrations of Bi–O–Bi and Nb–O–Nb [40]. In the spectrum of rGO in Fig. 3e, the band region around 2357 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of the C–H bond, whereas the peak that occurs at 1713 cm−1 corresponds to CO skeletal vibrations of carboxylic groups [41]. The peak that appeared at 1554 and 1203 cm−1 revealed the presence of sp2 stretching vibration of CC and of C–O groups respectively. The C–O–C bending vibration was confirmed by the peak occurring around 800 cm−1 [42]. The peak near 750 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups CO located at the edges of GO [43]. The 500-600 cm−1 band region reveals the narrow broadening of –OH stretching vibrations indicating the elimination of physisorbed water molecules located between the GO sheets. Physisorbed water means the water situated between the GO sheets that does not chemically interact with the oxygen functional groups of GO and can be easily removed at low temperatures without affecting the structural pattern of GO [44]. Hence, the FT-IR spectrum of rGO confirms its chemical structure. The small peaks found at 603, 693 and 929 cm−1 in BiNbO4, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO were assigned for stretching vibrations of Nb–O–Nb, Ti–O–Nb and O–Nb–O respectively [45]. The Ti–O–Nb vibrations recorded at 693 cm−1 suggested the surface interaction of Nb5+ with TiO2 lattice since the ionic radii of Nb5+ and Ti4+ are equal. The ionic radii of Nb5+ and Ti4+ were reported as 0.64 [46] and 0.68 Å [47] respectively. Hence it was expected that BiNbO4 nanocrystals were grown on the TiO2 surface via Ti–O–Nb and formed the heterojunctions between TiO2 and BiNbO4 during the impregnation process. Similarly, the presence of rGO on TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was confirmed with the FT-IR peaks found in the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO spectrum at 757,1531 and 2313 cm−1, which were attributed to stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups, CC and C–H bonds [41–43]. The above-discussed results confirmed the presence of BiNbO4 and rGO on TiO2 in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite.

The crystalline disorder and defects that occur in structure could be evaluated from raman spectra and the intensity ratio between D and G bands calculates the disorder that occurred in graphene derivatives. The Raman spectra of all synthesized TiO2, BiNbO4, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO are shown in Fig. 4 . The TiO2 spectrum, the observed vibrational modes at 146.55 cm−1 (Eg), 398.73 cm−1 (B1g), 517.84 cm−1 (A1g + B1g) and 640.59 cm−1 (Eg) denoted the anatase phase of TiO2 [48]. The characteristics of vibrational modes observed in the spectrum of BiNbO4 at 101.90 cm−1 (B1g), 144.20 cm−1 (Ag), 226.24 cm−1 (B3g), 277.69 cm−1 (F2g), 320.85 cm−1 (B3g), 368.65 cm−1 (Ag), 427.75 cm−1 (B3g), 524.66 cm−1 (Eg), 617.24 cm−1 (A1g), 688.04 cm−1 (B1g) and the stretching vibration band at 891.5 cm−1 confirmed the octahedron BiO6, these results confirmed the orthorhombic phase of BiNbO4 [49–51]. TiO2/BiNbO4 spectrum did not show any peak shifts and peaks related to BiNbO4 due to its low concentration loading. But, in the spectrum of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO, TiO2 peaks showed a slight blue shift due to the surface modification and the confinement effect that occurred in the nanocomposite, which results from the formation of surface defects such as oxygen vacancies and decreases in particle size (<10 nm) respectively [52]. The additional peaks obtained at 1303 and 1602 cm−1 in the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO spectrum were due to the D and G bands corresponding to the breathing mode of κ-point phonons of A1g and E2g of C sp2 atoms respectively, which revealed the existence of rGO in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO. However, the D band suggests a disorder linked to defects related to oxygen vacancies and grain boundaries [53,54]. The calculated relative intensity ratio between ID and IG of rGO in nanocomposite was 0.97 indicating the defects due to the presence of a reduced oxygenated functional group exists in graphene oxide.

The morphological nature of synthesized nanocomposite TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was characterized by HRSEM and HRTEM studies and shown in Fig. 5 . HRSEM images from Fig. 5a and b depicted the spherical-shaped particles of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO with an agglomeration. The atomic weight percentages of Ti, Bi, Nb, C, and O were found as 55.07, 01.12, 02.97, 13.83 and 27.01 %, respectively from EDX analysis and shown in Fig. 5c. The EDX measurements revealed the existence of TiO2, BiNbO4 and rGO in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposites. The weight percentage of Ti was more dominant than the other elements since TiO2 is the major component in the prepared TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite.

HRTEM analysis reassured the spherical morphology of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite particles in Fig. 5d. The particle size was evaluated by oddly choosing the nanoparticles from the images in Fig. 5e and the histogram image in Fig. 5g also showed the size distribution range of the synthesized TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite material. From the above results, the calculated average particle size of nanoparticles was around 9.8 nm. From the resolved lattice fringes in Fig. 5e, the d spacing between the fringes was calculated as 0.35 and 0.31 nm, which confirmed the presence of the anatase phase of TiO2 (101) and the orthorhombic phase of BiNbO4 (121) in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO. Hence the closely anchored TiO2 and BiNbO4 nanoparticles on the rGO sheet resulted in the heterojunctions. The superimposed crystal lattice structure shown in Fig. 5e might be due to the epitaxial growth of BiNbO4 on TiO2. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) analysis in Fig. 5f reveals the crystalline nature of nanoparticles, the multiple concentric rings around the center of the bright area demonstrated the polycrystalline nature of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO. Finally, it concludes that the successful construction of heterojunction between TiO2 and BiNbO4 was achieved with the contribution of rGO as a supporting material.

The nanocomposite TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was investigated by XPS analysis to study the chemical valence states and elements present in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO. It confirmed the presence of Ti (2p), O (1s), Bi (4f), Nb (3d) and C (1s) as shown in Fig. 6 . The presence of Ti4+ chemical states in the nanocomposite TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was confirmed with the deconvoluted peaks as shown in Fig. 6a with binding energies of 457.89 and 463.69 eV, which were attributed to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 spin-orbital splitting respectively. In Fig. 6b, the O1s spectrum confirmed the formation of Ti–O in the nanocomposite and the existence of residual oxygen group was highly evidenced by the distinct peak obtained with the binding energy of 530.05 and 531.85 eV respectively [47]. The deconvoluted peaks with binding energy at 165.8 and 160.4 eV were ascribed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 respectively, which confirmed the presence of Bi element in the nanocomposite shown in Fig. 6c. In Nb 3d spectra (Fig. 6d), the peaks observed with binding energies at 208.5 and 211 eV corresponded to Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2 [55,56]. The presence of rGO in the nanocomposite was confirmed by the C1s spectra with the prominent peak observed at 284.75 eV for C–C [47] and also with the shoulder peaks at 285.6 for C–O and 288.2 eV for O–CO as shown in Fig. 6e [47,57]. Hence, the presence of Ti, O, Bi, Nb and C was confirmed with the XPS results, which provided evidence for the successful fabrication of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite composed of TiO2, BiNbO4 and rGO.

The textural properties of TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO were obtained from the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm as shown in Fig. 7 a. The isotherm belongs to type IV with an H2 hysteresis loop shown in Fig. 7a, which exposes the presence of mesopores with the lengthy chain-like interconnected porous structure in TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite. The specific surface area for TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was estimated at 220, 222 and 229 m2/g. Using the adsorption branch of the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda method (BJH), the pore volume and pore size of TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO were acquired. The calculated average pore size of TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO from the pore size distribution curve shown in Fig. 7b were tabulated and shown in Table 1 . The results obtained from the BET analysis confirmed that the specific surface area of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was higher than bare TiO2. Since crystallite size decreases pore diameter and specific surface area increases, hence the photocatalytic efficacy was improved.

DRS UV–Visible absorption analysis confirms the optical properties of the synthesized photocatalyst. The spectrum displayed in Fig. 9a provides the data about the maximum light absorption of TiO2, BiNbO4, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO at 400, 422, 419 and 430 nm respectively. From the Tauc plots spectrum, the band gap energies of the photocatalyst TiO2, BiNbO4, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO are correspondingly calculated at 3.05, 2.88, 2.95 and 2.80 eV as shown in Fig. 9b. The estimated band gap energies revealed that light absorption of bare TiO2 was shifted to the visible region on the incorporation of BiNbO4 and rGO. To evaluate the mechanisms involved, the band edge position was required and obtained by using the Mulliken electronegativity formula as follows,
(1a) ECB = EVB - Eg
EVB = χ - Ee +0.5 Eg (2a)
Where, Ee - Energy of free electrons on H2 scale, EVB - Valence band edge potential, χ - Electronegativity, ECB - Conduction band edge potential and Eg - Band gap energy. The electronegativity values of TiO2 and BiNbO4 are 5.81 [47] and 6.26 eV [58] respectively. The conduction and valence band edge potentials of TiO2 were calculated at −0.22 eV (CB), +2.84 eV (VB) and for BiNbO4 +0.32 eV (CB) and +3.2 eV (VB) as shown in Fig. 8
. The estimated CB and VB edge potentials of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO are at −0.09 eV and 2.71 eV respectively. The electrons in the oxygen vacancies create the electronic energy levels below the CB position. The orbitals interaction between the 2p orbital of oxygen of TiO2 and the 2p orbital of carbon of rGO created energy levels above the VB position. Hence, the bottom of the CB position of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was lowered from −0.22 eV to −0.09 eV and the top of the VB position of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite rised from the +2.84 eV to +2.71 eV. Hence, the band gap of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was reduced from 3.05 eV (TiO2) to 2.80 eV (TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron separation and transportation (PL and EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: A photoluminescence study was carried out to investigate the efficiency in charge carrier separation of the synthesized TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanomaterials, which were scanned in the 300–600 nm range at room temperature as shown in Fig. 10 a. The predominant factors for the origin of the PL spectrum are attributed to oxygen vacancies, surface imperfections and excitons trapped within the material itself. In Fig. 10a, it was observed that loading of BiNbO4 and rGO on TiO2 does not create any light-emitting sites, hence no shift in the peak position of anatase TiO2 was observed for all the prepared nanomaterials due to the lower concentration of BiNbO4 and rGO. Synchronously, a noticeable decrease in the intensity of emission peaks was observed for TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO, which indicated an improved exciton lifetime. The presence of an emission peak at 378 nm that consistently appeared in all spectra indicates the band-to-band transition in TiO2 [59–61]. The peaks from 400 to 500 nm represent the defects related to oxygen vacancies [60]. The peak at 490 nm was attributed to the charge transfer process from Ti3+ to oxygen anion in TiO2 with surface oxygen vacancies [62]. The peaks at 435 nm in all spectra indicated the trapped electrons in oxygen vacancies. The peak observed at 435 nm was due to F or F2+ color centers for two electrons and no electrons trapped with oxygen vacancies respectively [63,64]. An appreciable decrease in the peak intensity of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was observed compared to TiO2/BiNbO4, which confirmed the role of rGO in suppressing e−/h+ recombination since the intensity of the PL peak is directly proportional to e−/h+ recombination. PL emissions observed implied the interaction between TiO2 with BiNbO4 and rGO and the defects that occur on the surface of TiO2 [59]. This result implies that rGO, an electron-accepting material facilitated high electron mobility with its planar structure and transported the electrons for the reduction of H+ ions. Hence the overall H2 production was improved in TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite.

The EIS spectra (shown in Fig. 10b) also depicted the charge separation efficiency and transfer resistance among the photogenerated electrons in the synthesized photocatalyst TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO. The displayed EIS Nyquist plots illustrated that TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite showed the least charge transfer resistance than TiO2/BiNbO4 and bare TiO2. The calculated resistance for TiO2, TiO2/BiNbO4 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO are 5.7, 4.3 and 3.8 Ω (ohm) respectively. These results further emphasized the role of rGO as a charge transporter to suppress the e−/h+ recombination and to improve the reduction of H+ ions. rGO matrix played a crucial role in charge carrier separation and electron mediator between TiO2 and BiNbO4 nanomaterials. Additionally, it also provided a better scaffold for the dispersed TiO2 and BiNbO4 nanoparticles in the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite, which enhanced the chemical stability of the photocatalyst. The acquired outcomes were well correlated and consistent with PL investigations.

EPR study was conducted at room temperature to understand the defects and oxygen vacancies on the surface of the synthesized materials and shown in Fig. 11 . The g-factor of TiO2 and TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was recorded as 2.002 and 2.003 respectively, which suggested the presence of trapped electrons and hole centers linked to the oxygen vacancies [65,66]. The increase in the intensity of the EPR signal of nanocomposite is much higher than that of TiO2, which demonstrates the increased electron-electron interactions due to the increased oxygen vacancies and the presence of p localized pi electrons of rGO [67]. The sharp signal was due to the localized spin related to the defects and edge effect at the heterojunction of TiO2 and BiNbO4. The broad signals might be due to the interaction of conduction electrons with the localized p electrons of the sp2 domains of rGO [68]. TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO with multiple unpaired electrons in the oxygen vacancies and the localized p electrons of rGO experiences more electron-electron interactions, which resulted a fine EPR signal with the increased intensity. These results confirmed the formation of heterojunction nanocomposite.

The photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized catalysts was assessed using a water-splitting technique under direct sunlight in an aqueous medium. The amount of H2 gas produced during the process was evaluated using gas chromatography analysis. From the results obtained, it was observed that the photocatalytic efficiency of the ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was higher than TiO2/BiNbO4, pristine TiO2 and BiNbO4. By optimizing the loading proportions of BiNbO4 and rGO on TiO2, the maximum amount of H2 evolution was achieved in the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite. Fig. 12 a depicts the performance of different wt % of BiNbO4 (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 wt %) loaded on TiO2, among them 1 wt % TiO2/BiNbO4 showed the maximum efficiency of 6730 μmol h−1 gcat −1 H2 production, whereas bare TiO2 (4820 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) and BiNbO4 (950 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) produced comparatively lower H2 production. This finding revealed the contribution of BiNbO4 to improve the efficiency of TiO2 by developing the heterojunction. The surface state energy levels at heterojunctions improved the interfacial charge transfer efficiency and suppressed the e−/h+ recombination rate. Additionally, BiNbO4 loading on TiO2 showed a shift in the light absorption from 400 to 419 nm with enhanced surface area, which improved visible light absorption and performance under sunlight. Further, the increase in the loading concentration beyond 1 wt % of BiNbO4 on TiO2 decreased the photocatalytic efficiency due to the inhibition of active pore sites. Hence TiO2/1 wt % of BiNbO4 showed higher efficiency due to the redshift with reduced e−/h+ recombination at the heterojunction. Following that the loading concentration of rGO on TiO2/1 wt % of BiNbO4 was optimized to achieve maximum efficiency, among the various wt % of rGO (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 wt %), 1 wt % rGO loaded TiO2/1 wt % of BiNbO4 nanocomposite showed the highest efficiency as shown in Fig. 12b. In the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO system, BiNbO4 and TiO2 nanocrystals were anchored on the solid-state supporting matrix rGO and facilitated the heterojunctions. The separated excited electrons at the heterojunction were easily transported via rGO with high electron mobility to the surface H+ ions reduction and hence the photocatalytic H2 production was enhanced. Additionally, TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO showed a further red shift from 419 to 430 nm to the visible region with enhanced specific surface area. The sequence of H2 production for various prepared photocatalysts was as follows, BiNbO4 (950 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) < TiO2 (4820 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) < TiO2/1 wt % BiNbO4 (6730 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) < TiO2/1 wt % BiNbO4/1 wt % rGO (8910 μ mol h−1 gcat −1) and the results are shown in Fig. 12c. Hence TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO became a visible light-responsive efficient photocatalyst for the H2 production by water splitting technique. This work confirmed the potential role of co-catalysts in improving the photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic performance of catalysts with and without co-catalysts was compared and shown in Table 2 .

The stability of the photocatalyst was determined by reusing the same photocatalyst with the aforementioned process at regular intervals as depicted in Fig. 12d. From the results, it was found that a consistent and acceptable decrease in H2 evolution over all the cycles and is shown in Fig. 12d. This result demonstrated the excellent stability and reusability of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO for the water-splitting process. The photocatalytic hydrogen production of synthesized TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO was compared with similar types of ternary nanocomposites reported in the literature and shown in Table 3 [65–72] since the newly developed TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was not yet reported for the photocatalytic applications.

The plausible electron pathway mechanism was proposed based on the characterization and the experimental results, which is shown in Fig. 13 . When TiO2 and BiNbO4 are exposed to direct sunlight, the electrons get excited to the CB position leaving the holes in the VB position with the formation of e−/h+ pairs.
(1b) (TiO2, BiNbO4) + hν → e− (TiO2, BiNbO4) + h+ (TiO2, BiNbO4)

The excited electrons participate in the H+ ions reduction and the holes are scavenged by the hole scavenger glycerol.
(2b) H2O + 2h+ (TiO2, BiNbO4) → ½ O2 + 2H+
(3) 2e− + 2H+ → H2
(4) 2H2O + 4hν → 2H2 + O2
(5) Glycerol (C3H8O3) + 14 h+ + 3H2O → 3CO2 + 14H+

Though the pristine BiNbO4 showed comparatively low H2 production due to its positive reduction potential, as a co-catalyst BiNbO4 in the TiO2/BiNbO4 heterostructure improved the performance of TiO2. The excited electrons of BiNbO4 scavenged the valence band holes of TiO2 via the Z-scheme electron pathway with the suitable CB position, hence the free excited electrons of TiO2 were available for the reduction reaction.

Subsequently, the holes present in VB of BiNbO4 were successfully neutralized by the hole scavenger glycerol. Here, glycerol readily reduces and donate electrons sufficiently which inhibits the e−/h+ pairs recombination and promotes the photocatalytic efficiency [73]. The surface state energy levels at the heterojunction between TiO2 and BiNbO4 in TiO2/BiNbO4 shifted the light absorption edge position to the visible region by narrowing the band gap of TiO2/BiNbO4. Hence the heterojunctions between TiO2 and BiNbO4 with Z-scheme mechanism played a crucial role in enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 under solar light. On anchoring TiO2/BiNbO4 on the rGO matrix, the excited electrons of TiO2 were transported to the surface reduction reaction via rGO due to its excellent electron-accepting and conducting properties.

Hence the overall performance of TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was improved by the fast charge transfer efficiency and suppressed e−/h+ recombination. Type II heterojunction of TiO2/BiNbO4 and rGO synergistically improved the performance of TiO2, hence the optimum loading of 1 wt % of BiNbO4 and 1 wt % of rGO on TiO2 (TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO) nanocomposite showed twofold H2 production than pristine TiO2 under direct sunlight exposure. The improved optical properties and specific surface area of all prepared nanomaterials were tabulated in Table 4 .

The ternary nanocomposite (TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO) was successfully fabricated by hydrothermal reaction followed by a wet impregnation process. The optimum of 1 wt % BiNbO4 and rGO loaded TiO2 nanocomposite showed the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution under natural solar light irradiation due to the reduced band gap energy with the increased surface area from 220 to 229 m2/g. The type II heterojunction between TiO2 and BiNbO4 increased the exciton life with enhanced interfacial charge transport. The Z-scheme mechanism facilitated the excited electrons in the CB of TiO2 for the reduction of H+ ions. These excited electrons were transported through the rGO matrix, to the surface reduction reaction. The fine EPR signal with increased intensity confirmed the defects at the heterojunctions, which reduced the band gap to absorb the light in the visible region. Hence, the ternary TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite showed an enhanced H2 production of about 8910 μ mol h−1 gcat −1 than the pristine TiO2 of 4820 μ mol h−1 gcat −1. BiNbO4 and rGO loading on TiO2 shifted the light absorption edge position from 400 to 430 nm, hence TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite worked as a visible responsive photocatalyst and showed enhanced photocatalytic performance under solar light. The stability of the TiO2/BiNbO4/rGO nanocomposite was confirmed by the recyclability studies. This work clearly revealed the importance of selecting the co-catalysts with suitable band alignment in fabricating efficient visible light-responsive nanocomposites.

A. Meera: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. M. Mahalakshmi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. V. Jesintha: Formal analysis. B. Neppolian: Resources.
